Brief Description: Picus is a Racket implementation of the QED^2 tool proposed in \cite{pailoor_automated_2023} and allows for checking the uniqueness property (that is, under-constrained signals) of \gls{zkp} circuits. Picus combines \gls{smt} solving (currently supported \gls{smt} solvers are cvc4, cvc5, and z3) with static analysis based on lightweight \gls{ucp} and currently supports three target files: \gls{r1cs} --- as \gls{ir} --- and Circom and gnark. Given a circuit, Picus returns one among three potential outputs: unknown (when Picus could not get a result within the given time limit), and safe or unsafe (when Picus found or did not find underconstrained bugs, respectively). If a circuit is unsafe, Picus also returns a counterexample showing that the circuit is underconstrained. Notably, Picus comes with a pre-built docker image as well as a complete documentation. Picus does not seem to be maintained anymore, as Veridise now offers a new version of Picus (Picus v2) within its AuditHub platform.

Picus v2\footnote{Picus Overview | AuditHub Documentation (\url{https://docs.veridise.com/picus-v2})} is the closed-source evolution of Picus, available only through AuditHub,\footnote{Home | AuditHub Documentation (\url{https://docs.veridise.com/})} Veridise's commercial SaaS (software-as-a-service) platform available to customers which hired Veridise for a security audit. Similarly to Picus, Picus v2 allows for checking the determinism of circuits by identifying underconstrained bugs using the same approach based on both static analysis and \gls{smt} solving. However, differently from Picus, Picus v2 operates over its own circuit constraint language (the Picus Constraint Language) and verifies circuits written in that language only (and contained in files with the ".picus" extension).

Brief Description: Circomspect is a static analyzer and linter for Circom circuits written in Rust.\footnote{It pays to be Circomspect - The Trail of Bits Blog (\url{https://blog.trailofbits.com/2022/09/15/it-pays-to-be-circomspect/})} In detail, Circomspect is based on the Circom compiler and uses the same parser as the compiler does. Circomspect is mainly based on data flow analyses and syntactic patterns matching, which are used to identify a variety of potential security vulnerabilities and common issues. In fact, Circomspect implements a number of analysis passes and targets underconstrained bugs (i.e., signal assignment, under-constrained signal, unused output signal, unconstrained less-than), over and underflow errors (i.e., non-strict binary conversion, bitwise complement, field element arithmetic, field element comparison), input validation errors (i.e., Unconstrained division), as well as concerns common to generic software (i.e., unused variables, branches with constant values, variables shadowing, and suggestions to overly complex split circuits). On the other hand, Circomspect's rich analysis has neither soundness nor completeness guarantees. In fact, all issues flagged by Circomspect do not represent vulnerabilities, but rather locations that should be reviewed to make sure that the circuit behaves as expected. Circomspect can be executed via command line in a terminal and currently supports only Circom files as target. Given a circuit, Circomspect outputs debug information, warnings, and errors to the terminal stdout. Circomspect does not have an UI, but its output can be written also into a Sarif file which can be visualized by the VSCode Sarif Viewer. Circomspect has a complete documentation, but its development appears to have stalled in 2024.

Pilspector is a security analysis tool based on \gls{smt} solving (using z3 as \gls{smt} solver) and written in Rust. In detail, Pilspector is based on the pilcom compiler\footnote{0xPolygon/pilcom: Polynomials Identity Language (\url{https://github.com/0xPolygon/pilcom})} and targets state machines written in \gls{pil}, a \gls{dsl} for developing and verifying programs within the \gls{zkEVM}.\footnote{Index - Polygon Knowledge Layer (\url{https://docs.polygon.technology/zkEVM/spec/pil/})} Pilspector mainly looks for signs of under-constrained circuits in \gls{pil} descriptions. Pilspector can be executed via command line in a terminal and currently supports only .pil and .pil.json files as target. Albeit providing test cases, Pilspector has neither a documentation nor a UI, and and its development appears to have stopped.

Brief Description: ZKAP is a set of 9 static analyzers for detecting bugs in Circom circuits, similar in scope and approach to Circomspect\footnote{whbjzzwjxq/ZKAP (\url{https://github.com/whbjzzwjxq/ZKAP})} --- though differences between ZKAP and Circomspect exist and are detailed in \cite{wen_practical_2024}. In detail, the design of ZKAP is based on a manual study of existing vulnerabilities in Circom; ZKAP is capable of abstracting Circom circuits as circuit dependence graphs to capture semantic vulnerability patterns. Consequently, ZKAP can identify vulnerabilities through semantic patterns matching, expressed in a Datalog-like language. As a result, ZKAP comprises 9 static analyzers, targeting underconstrained bugs (i.e., unconstrained signal, assignment misuse, unconstrained circuit output, unconstrained sub-circuit input, unconstrained sub-circuit output), semantic discrepancies in constraints computation (i.e., dataflow-constraint discrepancy, type mismatch, non-deterministic dataflow), and input validation errors (i.e., division-by-zero). We highlight that ZKAP and Circomspect target overlapping set of vulnerabilities, and that the same vulnerability may be named differently in ZKAP and Circomspect (e.g., division-by-zero in ZKAP practically corresponds to unconstrained division in Circomspect). Similarly to Circomspect, ZKAP cannot claim neither soundness nor completeness guarantees, although benchmarks show that ZKAP achieves a lower rate of false negatives and false positives than Circomspect \cite{wen_practical_2024}. ZKAP can be executed via command line in a terminal and currently supports only Circom files as target. Given a circuit, ZKAP outputs ...???. ZKAP comes with a docker image but has a rather essential documentation and its development appears to have stalled.

Targeted Vulnerabilities: secret data leakage, under-constrained circuits, input validation errors
Brief Description: ZK Vanguard is a static analyzer for detecting common bugs in circuits for \glspl{zkp}.\footnote{ZK Vanguard Overview | AuditHub Documentation (\url{https://docs.veridise.com/zkvanguard/})} Currently, ZK Vanguard supports circuits written in the LLZK \gls{ir}, thereby supporting \gls{zkp} schemes such as Circom, halo2, Plonky3, and Zirgen. In detail, ZK Vanguard comprises 8 different static analyzers targeting underconstrained bugs (i.e., unconstrained signal, underconstrained output, out-of-range signals, compute-constrain difference, signal-dependent control flow), secret data leakage (i.e., private input leakage), input validation errors (i.e., division-by-zero), as well as concerns common to generic software (i.e., unused fields). Further information (e.g., on soundness and completeness guarantees) is missing since, as of December 2025, the documentation of ZK Vanguard is still in writing. Finally, similarly to Picus v2, also ZK Vanguard is closed-source and available only through AuditHub. ZK Vanguard has a legacy version targeting Circom circuits only, ZK Vanguard (Circom),\footnote{ZK Vanguard (Circom) Overview | AuditHub Documentation (\url{https://docs.veridise.com/zkvanguard-legacy})}. However, ZK Vanguard (Circom) --- which is closed-source and available only through AuditHub --- will be deprecated in the near future.

Security Guarantees: randomized - output may vary, completeness (of analysis)
Brief Description: Proposed in \cite{hochrainer_fuzzing_2025}, Circuzz aims at discovering bugs in \gls{zkp} pipelines through fuzzing techniques and, specifically, metamorphic testing.\footnote{Rigorous-Software-Engineering/circuzz: Fuzzing processing pipelines for zero-knowledge circuits (\url{https://github.com/Rigorous-Software-Engineering/circuzz})} In detail, Circuzz operates by generating a configurable number of random circuits in an intermediate language --- named CircIL --- designed to capture a common subset of the aforementioned \glspl{dsl}. Currently, Circuzz supports 4 \glspl{dsl}: Circom, Noir, gnark, and Corset. However, the generality of CircIL ideally allows to easily extend Circuzz to new \glspl{dsl} and \gls{zkp} pipelines. Once random circuits are generated, Circuzz applies metamorphic equivalence transformations to obtain semantically equivalent but syntactically different circuits. Finally, Circuzz runs the obtained circuits through the rest of the pipeline --- lowering (compilation), setup, prove, and verify. If two equivalent circuits yields different outputs or behaviours, a bug is found. Notably, Circuzz also introduced metamorphic transformations on the pipeline settings (e.g., compilers optimization levels). Differently from the majority of the other security analysis tools, Circuzz aims at discovering bugs in the components (e.g., compilers, witness generators, provers, verifiers) of a pipeline, and not on specific circuits. In particular, such bugs can expose two types of critical issues, namely soundness and completeness concerns. Being based on metamorphic testing, Circuzz is sound (i.e., does not produce false positives) but has no completeness guarantees. Also, the randomness intrinsic in its fuzzing approach makes Circuzz not deterministic (i.e., different runs may produce different outputs). Circuzz has a complete documentation and the last update to its codebase was on June 2025.

Brief Description: Proposed in \cite{takahashi_zkfuzz_2026}, zkFuzz is a Rust fuzzer for identifying security vulnerabilities in circuits for \gls{zkp} through mutation testing.\footnote{Koukyosyumei/zkFuzz: ZK Circuit Fuzzer (IEEE S&P'26) (\url{https://github.com/Koukyosyumei/zkFuzz})} zkFuzz leverages a novel theoretical formulation --- called \gls{tcct} --- for modeling various kinds of vulnerabilities in circuits, in particular both under- and over-constrained vulnerabilities. In detail, zkFuzz operates by mutating both inputs and witness generators to detect \gls{tcct} violations in circuits. Currently, zkFuzz supports Circom and Noir, albeit support for additional \glspl{dsl} is feasible and planned. Interestingly, zkFuzz has an extension, zkFuzz++, which leverages static analysis to identify potential vulnerabilities and guide the mutation testing. Essentially, zkFuzz++ considers code smells for Circom circuits such as, e.g., assignments without constraints and division-by-zero pattern. Being based on mutation testing, zkFuzz is sound (i.e., does not produce false positives) but has no completeness guarantees. Also, the randomness intrinsic in its fuzzing approach makes zkFuzz not deterministic (i.e., different runs may produce different outputs).

Similar in scope and approach to Circuzz, Aztec_fuzzing is a set of Rust tools for fuzzing the compiler of the Noir \gls{dsl}.\footnote{FuzzingLabs/aztec_fuzzing (\url{https://github.com/FuzzingLabs/aztec_fuzzing})} In detail, Aztec_fuzzing aims to randomly generate Noir code and then check the Noir compiler's behavior to detect potential security vulnerabilities. Unfortunately, the documentation of Aztec_fuzzing is limited and no details are provided concerning targeted vulnerabilities or experimental results (e.g., bugs found).

Brief Description: cLean is a domain-specific language (DSL) for circuits developed by zkSecurity targeting AIR. Technically, the cLean language is deeply embedded in the Lean4 proof assistant, and implements an infrastructure for mathematically proving the correctness of circuits with respect to a given formal specification written in Lean4 itself. The development also includes prototypical tooling for extracting a Plonky3\footnote{https://github.com/Plonky3/Plonky3} arithmetization of a circuit. Establishing that a cLean circuit adheres to a specification can be quite challenging because of the substantial manual effort required to prove properties in the Lean4 proof assistant, however once a property is established the circuit can be treated as a black box. Documentation is rather scarce but an AI-generated wiki\footnote{https://deepwiki.com/Verified-zkEVM/clean} exists, along with some instructions on how to prove theorems. Also, the project does not ship with a Docker image, but the Lean4 tooling makes it easy to setup and run cLean.

Ecne is a library and CLI tool that performs a static analysis on R1CS constraint systems. Given an unoptimized R1CS, obtained, e.g., by compiling a circom circuit with option \verb|-O0|, the tool produces a report on the command line detailing whether the R1CS is underconstrained. Interestingly, in addition to a CLI interface and library APIs, Ecne also provides a REST API for invoking its analysis. Technically, the tool repeatedly applies nine specialized rules to (a slightly modified version of) the given R1CS system to check if all the variables therein are uniquely determined by the inputs. As of December 2025, the source code of Ecne is available on GitHub\footnote{https://github.com/franklynwang/EcneProject} under the GPLv3 licence, however the tool seems to be unmaintained and the documentation is scarce.

Brief Description: Coda is a functional programming language embedded in OCaml and endowed with a refinement type system, which is used to write the circuit specification. Intuitively, if a Coda circuit typechecks its semantics is equivalent to that specified by the type of said circuit. Being the type checking of Coda intractable, some of the constraints generated during type checking are translated into Rocq lemmas and need to be proved separately, using a library of tactics provided by Coda to ease proof automation. Coda also ships with a compiler that produces R1CS files compatible with different backends. As of December 2025, Coda is open source and available on GitHub\footnote{https://github.com/Veridise/Coda}, but apparently not maintained anymore. The documentation for the tool is rather scarce, but the paper by~\citet{liu_certifying_2024} gives a detailed account of the theory and the evaluation methodology of the tool.

Brief Description: Rocq-of-Noir is a tool developed by Formal lands that translates Noir smart contracts into Rocq code. Rocq-of-noir provides a semantics of Noir in Rocq and tools for reasoning about programs. The tool is documented, provides installation instructions, code examples and a 2-part tutorial for getting started with Noir verification. However, verifying non trivial examples requires a significant amount of work and expertise in Rocq and formal verification. As of november 2025, Rocq-of-Noir seems maintaned and is open source under dual-licensing, MIT and Apache 2.0.

Brief Description: Lampe implements a model of the Noir language within the Lean4, with the aim of supporting the verification efforts of the Noir language itself and its programs. In its current form Lampe can automatically extract a Lean4 model corresponding to the given Noir program, whose correctness wrt some specification can be then established within Lean4. Currently, however, the project does not seem to provide automation facilities like others and seem to rely entirely on the abilities of the proof engineer. Lampe is currently maintained and released under the Apache 2.0 licence, its source code is available on GitHub\footnote{https://github.com/reilabs/lampe}. The repository also includes installation instructions and usage examples.

Brief Description: The gnark-lean-extractor compiles circuits from gnark to Lean4, enabling their formal verification in an interactive environment. The extractor presents itself as a Go library that integrates directly with gnark and provides a function \verb|CircuitToLean| that returns a string containing the circuit in a Lean4-compatible format using Reilabs' ProvenZK library.\footnote{https://github.com/reilabs/proven-zk} The tool is documented and based on well known libraries, released under an Apache 2.0 licence. The code is available on GitHub\footnote{https://github.com/reilabs/gnark-lean-extractor}.

Brief Description: The tool report~\cite{coglio_compositional_2023} reports the experience of the authors in formally verifying the R1CS constraint systems generated by the Aleo's SnarkVM\footnote{https://github.com/ProvableHQ/snarkVM} using the ACL2 prover. More in detail, they formalize SnarkVM R1CS constructions and prove that they adhere the given specifications. One of the most interesting aspects of their work is that their approach is compositional and, once proved correct, many ``components'' can be assembled together into larger ones, composing their correctness proofs. Apparently, their development is not open source, and only sketched in the paper.

Security Guarantees: determinism - always same output, soundness (of analysis), completeness (of analysis)
Brief Description: \citet{CMS} is a report collecting various experiences of the authors on mechanizing zero-knowledge verification with the ACL2 proof assistant. Their contributions include the formalization of finite-field arithmetic in ACL2, formalization of (a generalization of) R1CS constraints, facilities for proving constraints correctness with respect to a specification, and an integration with the Axe toolkit\footnote{https://www.kestrel.edu/research/axe/} within ACL2. With this infrastructure in place they were able to discover two new bugs and several optimizations in a zero-knowledge circuit construction library. The development is available as part of the Axe toolkit, and very well documented.

Brief Description: Garden is a formal verification framework within the Rocq proof assistant that allows to prove determinism, functional correctness, and completeness of circuits expressed in Garden itself. The translation of a circuit into the Garden's formalism is done by hand and it should be manually proved to be faithful to the original code. As usual with these systems all the proofs are checked by Rocq and can be assumed to be correct as long as the Rocq kernel is correct and no invalid axioms have been introduced. The whole development is open source, available on GitHub\footnote{https://github.com/formal-land/garden} and released under MIT licence by Formal Land. The repository includes build instructions and some examples on how to formally verify a circuit.

Circom-mutator\footnote{https://github.com/aviggiano/circom-mutator} is a mutation testing tool that targets Circom circuits. In particular it generates mutations of the given circuit by rewriting (parts of) it, line-by-line, in order to generate mutations that are intended to make circuit tests fail. The repository includes some documentation and an example of usage, and the whole project is released under the MIT open source licence. Unfortunately, circom-mutator appears to be unmaintained as of November 2025.

Brief Description: CCC-check is a language-agnostic static analyzer. It takes as inputs circuits written in the CirC intermediate representation and uses  abstract interpretation to detect bugs, including input validation errors (e.g., division-by-zero, array bounds errors), underconstrainedness and some others. The tool is released under the MIT licence and it is maintained.

Horus is a command-line analysis tool for Cairo programs targeting the StarkNet smart-contract ecosystem. Horus expects developers to add annotations (e.g. pre- and post-conditions, storage updates) that describe intended behaviours to Cairo programs. The Horus compiler then produces a specification file plus a compiled contract, and the Horus checker uses SMT solvers (like z3 or cvc5) to attempt to verify that the implementation obeys those annotations --- or else produce a counterexample (or runs out of time).
It is more focused on the program than the ZKP.

Medjai is an analysis tool for reasoning about Cairo programs based on symbolic execution. Developers express intended correctness properties via integrated Cairo or [V] specifications\footnote{Veridise/V: semantics and tooling for the [V] specification language. (\url{https://github.com/Veridise/V})}. Then, Medjai uses symbolic execution to explore all possible program states plus \gls{smt} solving to either prove those properties or produce counterexamples --- and also find concrete inputs that can crash Cairo programs.
It is more focused on the program than the ZKP.

Kinds of Output:
Textual output
Image (data and call flow graphs)
Brief Description:
Sierra Analyzer is a toolkit for Sierra including various analysis tools based on symbolic execution and static analysis. In detail, Sierra Analyzer provides a call graph generator, a control-flow graph generator, a decompiler, and informational and security detectors. Unfortunately, the documentation does not describe exactly which vulnerabilities each detector finds. Moreover, Sierra Analyzer is no longer maintained.
Supports fetching contracts from Starknet and Scarb projects.
It is more focused on the program than the ZKP.

Kinds of Output:
Textual output
Image (data and call flow graphs)
Brief Description:
A Python toolkit for Sierra based on symbolic execution and static analysis. Thoth is similar to Sierra Analyzer (more precisely, Sierra Analyzer was developed as a more maintainable version of Thoth's Sierra analysis CLI), even though Thoth remains a separate and broader toolkit. In fact, Thoth provides disassemblers and decompilers as well as generators for call graphs, control-flow graphs, and data-flow graphs. Notably, Thoth can generate inputs for Cairo-fuzzer. Like Sierra Analyzer, also Thoth is no longer maintained.
It is more focused on the program than the ZKP.

A fuzzing analysis tool for testing (compiled) Cairo programs. Unfortunately, Cairo-Fuzzer does not document target vulnerabilities, and it is no longer maintained.
It can be used as an independent tool or as a library.
It is more focused on the program than the ZKP.

Amarna is a static analyzer for Cairo. It has a set of rules (extendable) used to search for code patterns of interest, which may be security vulnerabilities or simply security-sensitive operations that need to be analyzed with greater attention when reviewing code. Amarna exports its static analysis results to the SARIF format, that can be easily integrated into VSCode to view warnings underlined in the code.
The development has stopped and seems to be superseded by Caracal.
It is more focused on the program than the ZKP.

Caracal is a static analyzer tool over the SIERRA representation for Starknet smart contracts. It allows to analyze Cairo code, the aim is to identify vulnerable and security-sensitive code. It advertises detectors for vulnerable Cairo code, printers to report information, and ease of usage within Scarb projects. Supports taint analysis and data flow analysis framework.
It is more focused on the program than the ZKP.

Primary Analysis Approach: fuzzing and metamorphic/mutation testing, dynamic analysis and instrumentation
Brief Description: SnarkFuzzer is a dynamic analysis tool and fuzzer based on mutation testing techniques for various \gls{r1cs}-based \glspl{zksnarks}, focusing on the runtime stages of the \gls{zkp} pipeline. SnarkFuzzer targets implementation-level vulnerabilities, including cryptographic logic errors, software bugs, and arithmetic errors like under- and overflows. Additionally, SnarkFuzzer checks cryptographic computations against ``trusted libraries''.

ConstraintChecker relies on \gls{smt} solving (z3) to verify the consistency of the \gls{r1cs} produced by the compiler of the \gls{r1cs}-based \glspl{zksnarks} programming tool being used with the corresponding user-specified set of proof statement equations. In other words, ConstraintChecker verifies that the circuit generation process generates the same proof as the developer states, finding any errors in flattening and/or gadget use.

Kinds of Output:
List of killed mutations
List of survided mutations
Brief Description: Hunter is a source-level mutation testing tool for Noir that assesses how effective a project's Noir unit tests are at catching real logic errors. Hunter parses Noir source files (with inline #[test]s), systematically (and deterministically) mutates operators and expressions, recompiles each mutant with the Noir toolchain, and runs the tests to see which mutations are detected (“killed”) and which silently pass (“survive”). The result is a mutation score and a report listing surviving mutants, which highlight parts of the circuit specification that are poorly tested or under-specified. However, Hunter does not analyze the underlying constraint system or proving backend, and it does not provide formal guarantees about soundness or determinism of the circuit. The tool is open-source and implemented as a Rust CLI on GitHub (nfurfaro/hunter). The tool is currently in its alpha stage of development (maybe forever).

Developers:
Shanghai Jiao Tong University
Shanghai Normal University
Brief Description:
An algebraic solver for Circom and Halo2 circuits \cite{chen_ac4_2024}. AC\textsuperscript{4} converts a circuit into polynomial equations (over the chosen field) which then analyzes with linear algebra and Gr\"obner-bases techniques --- which can be seen as a specialized form of constraint solving --- to determine whether the circuit is under- or over-constrained based on the number of algebraic solutions. 
AC\textsuperscript{4}  strength comes from the fact that Circom circuits are mostly linear, which is why the method works well there, but this advantage should not translate well to PLONKish arithmetization. 
Unfortunately, AC\textsuperscript{4} seems closed-source and no implementation is available.

Zequal is a static analysis tool developed by Veridise that checks whether a Circom circuit’s witness-generation semantics and its constraint system are consistent with each other (it cannot run on an IR, since it requires access to the witness logic). It operates directly on Circom source code, symbolically interprets both the generator logic and the constraint construction, and uses SMT solving to detect inputs where the two diverge, indicating integration errors in the specification. The analysis is sound (no false positives) but incomplete. Unfortunately, the tool is (currently) closed-source and no public implementation is available.

\mbnote{It is probably borderline symbolic execution, but I'd say this is a static analysis, because the tool is sound and indeed they seem to be overapproximating the behaviour of the product program using the symbolic storage and other tricks. }

ConsCS is a research-grade static analysis tool for Circom circuits that operates directly on compiled R1CS files and aims to detect under-constrained behavior. The tool combines a lightweight abstract-interpretation pass, implemented through a "Binary Property Graph", with SMT solving: the BPG infers simple properties such as booleanity, equalities, and range constraints, propagating them through the circuit to prune the search space before any solver is invoked. This preprocessing reduces the complexity of the SMT queries that follow and should improve solver success rates compared to other approaches. ConsCS is useful as a proof-of-concept for inference-guided R1CS verification, but the implementation is bare-bones and strictly research-oriented. It has no structural checks, weak diagnostics, and almost no documentation. The paper describing it is also paywalled, so the repo is effectively the only accessible version of the work.

Kinds of Output:
Provide counterexamples
Provide unsatisfied pre/post conditions
Brief Description: CIVER is an analysis tool based on \gls{smt} solving for Circom circuits \cite{isabel_scalable_2024}. CIVER runs on the Circom source code before any proving takes place and checks whether the circuit is correctly constrained. The tool uses an \gls{smt} solver for two kinds of analysis: (1) it checks user-written tags and pre/post conditions to make sure the circuit really enforces the intended rules and behavior, and (2) it tests determinism by trying to find two different valid witnesses for the same input, which would reveal an under-constrained circuit. Any issue the tool reports is a real defect (no false positives), but the analysis is not complete. Its output is a report showing which semantic checks failed and, when weak-safety breaks, concrete counterexamples demonstrating the non-deterministic behavior.

Korrekt is a static analysis tool for Halo2 circuits \cite{soureshjani_automated_2023}. It runs after the circuit is lowered (i.e., after synthesis builds the PLONKish constraint system in memory) and checks whether the circuit is fully and correctly constrained before setup or proving occur. The tool performs two types of analysis: (1) A fast structural pass that scans the constraint system to find obvious, guaranteed bugs (i.e. unused gates, unconstrained cells, and unused columns) and (2) an SMT-based search for actual under-constrained behavior by attempting to find two different witnesses that satisfy all constraints for the same public inputs. Any issue the tool reports is a real defect (no false positives), but the analysis is not complete. The output is a diagnostic report of definite structural issues and, when found, concrete counterexamples showing non-determinism in the circuit.
